title: Game 27: J. Lautier vs A. Shirov
fullText:
Positional Sacrifices

It is easy to understand a sacrifice that has an immediate concrete result – it leads to mate, or to the recovery of the material with interest, for example. However, it is also possible to sacrifice material on a longer-term basis. In this case, the compensation usually consists of positional factors – for example, the creation of a permanent weakness in the enemy position, or the exposure of the enemy king in a way which can eventually be exploited. It is often hard to judge the soundness of such positional sacrifices, since both tactical and positional factors are involved. We have already seen a good example of a positional sacrifice in Game 20, where Kasparov sacrificed the exchange in order to create a network of weak light squares in his opponent’s position.

Since the exploitation of positional advantages usually takes some time, positional sacrifices often occur in positions where the opponent has difficulty generating counterplay. The following game represents a marginal case.

J. Lautier – A. Shirov

Interzonal tournament, Manila 1990

King’s Indian Defence, Fianchetto Variation

1 d4

1...Nf6

2 Nf3

2...g6

For comments on the moves up to here, see Game 24.

3 g3

A solid move. Now that Black is committed to fianchettoing his king’s bishop, White does likewise, counting on his extra move to give him the edge. As with all symmetrical lines, Black must take care not to carry on the mimicry for too long, as he might end up with a depressingly passive position. See also Game 17.

3...Bg7

4 Bg2

4...0-0

For the time being, both sides content themselves with natural developing moves, which will have to be played sooner or later in any case.

5 0-0

The symmetry continues, but now Black has completed his kingside development. Before he can bring his queenside pieces into play, he must decide how to react in the centre.

5...d6

Shirov definitely decides to break the symmetry. The main alternative is 5...d5, when after 6 c4 it is risky for Black to play the symmetrical 6...c5. Instead, 6...dxc4 and 6...c6 are popular and solid options.

6 c4

Now the game has transposed into the King’s Indian Defence, Fianchetto Variation (so-called because White develops his bishop at g2). As in virtually all lines of the King’s Indian, sharp play can result.

6...Nc6

This move may look a little odd, because it blocks the move ...c5, which is often a means of challenging White’s centre in the Fianchetto King’s Indian. Moreover, White can simply kick the knight away with d5. However, there is considerable logic behind Black’s move and it is in fact the second most common in this position. Black’s idea is to develop flexibly for the moment. He may decide to challenge in the centre with ...e5, or he may play on the queenside with ...a6, ...Rb8 and ...b5, aiming to exchange his b-pawn for White’s more central c-pawn. In this latter case, any activity by Black in the centre will be delayed for several moves. Since White’s set-up is based more on soundness than aggression, Black can afford the time for this rather leisurely plan of counterplay.

The main alternatives are 6...Nbd7, followed by ...e5, 6...c6, aiming for piece-play with ...Qa5 and (probably) ...Be6, while 6...c5, directly attacking White’s centre, is another major option. All these lines are perfectly playable and, objectively speaking, there is little to choose between them.

7 Nc3

White simply continues his development, bringing his queen’s knight out to its most natural square.

7...a6

This is the most common move nowadays, as the plan of queenside activity is thought to be the best follow-up to ...Nc6. The alternative 7...e5 8 d5 Ne7 9 e4 should favour White. Play often develops into a traditional King’s Indian queenside vs kingside battle, but White’s fianchetto structure makes his kingside harder to attack than in similar lines with the bishop on e2.

8 h3

This flexible and useful little move prevents both ...Ng4 and ...Bg4. The former is significant if White develops his dark-squared bishop at e3, while the latter is important if White wants to play e4. The immediate 8 e4, for example, is inaccurate because of 8...Bg4 9 h3 Bxf3 10 Bxf3 Nd7 11 Be3 e5, when Black establishes a knight at d4. There are two other popular ideas for White. The first is the direct 8 d5 Na5 9 Nd2 c5 10 Qc2, when Black aims to attack the c4-pawn with ...a6, ...Rb8 and ...b5. The second is 8 b3, which avoids spending a tempo on h3 because it isn’t necessary if the bishop is developed on b2.

8...Bd7

This is a slightly unusual move. Black normally continues 8...Rb8, in order to play ...b5 as soon as possible. In this line he often leaves his bishop on c8 for some time since it is not yet clear where it will be best placed. It is true that d7 is very often the bishop’s destination, but Black need not commit it so soon.

9 Bg5

Lautier avoids the standard lines 9 e4 and 9 Be3, and finds a very unusual move. White’s idea is to induce ...h6, and then retreat his bishop to e3. After a later Qd2, Black will have to spend a tempo defending his h-pawn and White will have effectively gained a tempo over the line 9 Be3.

9...h6

This is not the only possible move, but it is logical to drive the bishop away from its active position on g5.

10 Be3

10...Rb8

Black continues just as if the pawn were on h7. He intends ...b5, exchanging his b-pawn for the more central c4-pawn.

11 Nd5

After 11 Qd2 Black can reply 11...Kh7 12 Nd5 Ne4 (12...b5 is also playable) 13 Qc2 f5 with a satisfactory position. If White continues sharply by 14 Nh4!? e6 15 Bxe4 fxe4 16 Nf4, then 16...Rxf4! (16...g5? loses to 17 Nxe6) 17 Bxf4 g5 18 Qxe4+ Kg8 19 d5 exd5 20 Qxd5+ Kh7 leads to perpetual check.

11...b5!?

A consistent and very combative move. Black does not mind having his pawns broken up if he can generate good piece activity. The quieter 11...e6 12 Nxf6+ Qxf6 13 Qd2 leaves White with a small but safe positional advantage after 13...Kh7 14 Rac1 Qe7 15 d5 Ne5 16 Nd4 or 13...g5 14 Rac1.

12 Nxf6+

White accepts the offer to double Black’s pawns. This exchange is virtually forced sooner or later, as White’s d5-knight was being undermined.

12 Qd2 was played in a later game Timman-Shirov, Donner Memorial, Amsterdam 1995. Black obtained a satisfactory position after 12...bxc4 13 Nxf6+ exf6 14 Bxh6 Bxh6 15 Qxh6 Rxb2 16 Nh4 Ne7 and now Timman decided to force a draw by 17 Bd5 Nxd5 18 Nxg6 fxg6 19 Qxg6+ Kh8 20 Qh6+ with a repetition of moves.

12...exf6

In this position the doubled pawns constitute a weakness, albeit a slight one. It is not so much that the pawns are weak in themselves; it is more that without an e-pawn Black will find it hard to challenge White’s main central strongpoint, the d4-pawn. In compensation, the e-file is opened for Black’s rook and Black gains a little time, since White has exchanged a knight that has made three moves for a knight that has only moved once.

13 cxb5

13 d5 Ne7 14 c5 is over-aggressive; after 14...Nf5 Black stands well.

13...Rxb5

Again, Black prefers activity over structure. It might seem more solid to play 13...axb5 keeping his queenside pawns together. The problem is that after 14 d5 Black has no good reply. For example, after 14...Ne5 (14...Ne7 15 Nd4 followed by Rc1 also starts to exert pressure against Black’s queenside) 15 Nd2, followed by Rc1, Black faces problems both with his queenside and with the almost trapped knight on e5. Thus playing 13...axb5 doesn’t really solve Black’s problems on the queenside, and offers no piece activity in compensation.

14 Qd2

14 Qc1!? might be slightly more accurate. If Black plays 14...g5 (as in the game) then White can reply 15 Nxg5 fxg5 16 Bxc6 Bxc6 17 Qxc6 (17 a4 Rb6 18 a5 Rb4 19 Qxc6 Qc8 is unclear) 17...Rxb2 18 Qxa6 Qa8 19 Qc4 with a slight advantage.

14...g5

14...Kh7 is also possible, but Shirov prefers to reduce the activity of White’s dark-squared bishop.

15 d5!

If White plays passively, then Black will continue ...Ne7-d5, occupying an excellent central square and preparing active play by ...f5. 15 Rac1 and 15 Ne1, for example, are both met by 15...Ne7 with satisfactory play for Black.

15 a4 is a critical move. Then:

1) 15...Rb8 16 d5 Ne7 (16...Ne5 17 Rfc1 also gives White an edge) 17 Nd4 leaves Black a little passively placed.

2) 15...Rb3! is more active. After 16 d5 Ne5 17 Rfc1 (17 Nd4 Nc4 18 Qc2 Nxe3 19 Qxb3 Nxf1 20 Rxf1 f5 activates the dark-squared bishop with a satisfactory position for Black) 17...Qb8 Black has sufficient counterplay.

15...Ne7

15...Ne5 16 Rac1! (16 Nd4?! Nc4 17 Qc3 Nxe3 18 fxe3 f5 is fine for Black) 16...Nxf3+ 17 exf3 f5 18 b3 is uncomfortable for Black. White will play f4, when it is not clear what compensation Black has for his weak queenside pawns.

16 Nd4

The natural follow-up. White has to move his knight in order to defend the d5-pawn, and after any other knight move Black achieves a good position with ...Nf5.

16...Rxd5!?

This sacrifice is the point behind Black’s play. Passively retreating the rook by 16...Rb8 would be an admission of failure; White would obtain an advantage with the natural move 17 Rac1.

17 Bxd5

17...Nxd5

Black has given up the exchange in return for a pawn. His compensation is not in the form of an immediate attack against the king, or any particularly strong threats. It is of a long-term nature, and is primarily positional rather than tactical. Such sacrifices are usually called ‘positional sacrifices’, although there is of course no clear-cut dividing line between positional sacrifices and those aiming for shorter-term compensation. Looking at the diagram, what has Black gained in return for his (admittedly fairly modest) material deficit? Firstly, he has eliminated White’s cramping central pawn, which both restrained his own c-pawn and created a possible outpost on c6. Secondly, he has removed White’s light-squared bishop. This not only gives White the immediate problem of dealing with the h3-pawn, but also raises the possibility that the weak light squares around the white king might eventually enable Black to start a direct attack.

Despite these advantages, the game is far from plain sailing for Black. His main problem is the vulnerability of his c- and d-pawns to attack along the half-open files. White’s rooks are well placed to swing into action, and Black could easily find himself facing strong pressure. Time is clearly an important factor here. If White has time to complete his development without Black having generated any active play, then Black will be forced back into a defensive posture. Therefore, both sides must try to make every tempo count. Objectively speaking, I believe that White has some advantage, but the resulting highly complex positions suit Shirov much better than Lautier, so while the balance of the position may still favour White, he is facing a tough challenge.

18 Nf5!

Lautier also knows how to play for the initiative, and is willing to offer his h-pawn in order to speed his rooks onto the central files. The alternatives are:

1) 18 Kh2 Qa8 (18...Nxe3? 19 fxe3! Qc8 20 g4 is bad, as White’s grip on f5 prevents Black from activating his dark-squared bishop) 19 Nc2 c5 keeps White’s pieces out of d4, and affords Black good compensation. He can continue with ...Re8, ...Bc6 and ...f5 (in some order) with plenty of activity, while White’s minor pieces have little scope.

2) 18 g4 Qc8 19 f3 (otherwise Black can at any rate force a draw by ...Bxg4) 19...Nxe3 20 Qxe3 f5 and with two active bishops against a knight and a weakened white kingside, Black is at least equal.

18...Bxf5

Forced, because 18...Nxe3? 19 fxe3! sets up a blockade on f5.

19 Qxd5

19...Bxh3

20 Rfd1

20 Rfc1 is inferior. After 20...Be6 21 Qb7 f5 22 Rxc7 Bd5! 23 Qxd5 (23 Qa7 f4 24 Bb6 Qf6 gives Black dangerous threats, while after 23 Qb6 f4 24 Rd1 fxe3 25 Rxd5 exf2+ 26 Kxf2 Qf6+ 27 Kg2 Qe6 28 Rd2 Qe4+ 29 Kh2 Be5 White’s kingside is looking exposed) 23...Qxc7 it is up to White to demonstrate full equality.

20...Re8

Pinning the e3-bishop against the undefended e2-pawn.

21 Rac1

This natural move completes White’s development. The alternative 21 Rd2 f5 22 Bd4 Bxd4 23 Qxd4 Qa8 24 f3 looks approximately equal.

21...f5

Once again, Black must act quickly or else White will converge on the weak c7-pawn. This move not only threatens the b2-pawn, but also starts a general pawn advance in the direction of White’s king.

22 Rd2

This move defends the e2- and b2-pawns in preparation for Bd4, and with very accurate play should give White an advantage. However, White can also gain an advantage with the simpler 22 Qc6. Then:

1) 22...f4 23 gxf4 gxf4 24 Bxf4 Bxb2 25 Rc4 is inadequate as Black is unable to exploit White’s slightly exposed king position. Note that 25...Rxe2? fails to 26 Qf3.

2) 22...Rxe3!? 23 fxe3 Qe7 is dubious here as White effectively has an extra tempo compared to the game. Although there are some traps to be negotiated, White should gain the advantage:

2a) 24 Rd3 Bxb2 25 Rc2 (25 Qxc7 Qe4 is good for Black) 25...Be5 26 Qxc7 Qf6 gives Black good play; for example, 27 Qc8+ (27 Kh2 g4 is similar) 27...Kg7 28 Kh2 g4 29 Rd5 (29 Qe8? Bxg3+! 30 Kxg3 Qa1 wins for Black) 29...h5 with a dangerous attack.

2b) 24 Kf2! Be5 (24...Bxb2 25 Qxc7 is very good for White) 25 Rd3 (25 Qxc7?? Bxg3+ mates) leaves Black struggling for compensation; for example, 25...Qf6 (25...h5 26 Qxc7 Qf6 27 Qd7 h4 28 gxh4 g4 29 Rc8+ Kg7 30 Qd8 defends) 26 Qe8+ Kg7 27 Rxc7 f4 28 exf4 gxf4 29 gxf4 Qxf4+ 30 Ke1 Qf1+ 31 Kd2 Bf4+ 32 Re3 and Black will end up the exchange for a pawn down.

3) 22...Bxb2 23 Rc2 Be5 24 Qxc7 and now:

3a) 24...Qa8 25 Qc6 Qxc6 (25...f4 26 gxf4 gxf4 27 Qxa8 Rxa8 28 Bd4 is also slightly better for White) 26 Rxc6 Bxg3 27 Bd4 and White has the better ending.

3b) 24...Qxc7 25 Rxc7 Bxg3 (25...f4 26 gxf4 gxf4 27 Bd4 favours White) 26 Rd3 Be5 27 Bxg5 Bg4 (27...hxg5 28 Rxh3 and Black’s a-pawn is liable to be lost within a few moves) 28 Re7 (28 Bxh6 Bxe2 29 Re3 Bb5 30 Rf3 Kh7 31 Bf4 Kg6 is drawish) 28...Rc8 29 Bxh6 Bxe2 30 Rh3 and White retains a slight advantage. It is true that Black has two pawns for the exchange, but his pawns are weak and isolated. White is better, but the general reduction in the number of pawns means that Black retains fair drawing chances.

22...Rxe3!?

This second sacrifice ups the stakes. Black is willing to invest more material to increase his positional assets. Note that again the sacrifice does not lead to an immediate attack on the white king, so this could again be described as a ‘positional’ sacrifice. In this case, however, the possibility of an attack is closer than hitherto, since White’s shattered pawns and the ominous presence of the h3-bishop pose an obvious danger to the king.

23 fxe3

23...Qe7

24 Kf2!

Black has sufficient counterplay after the alternatives, e.g. 24 Qd3 Be5 25 Kh2 Bg4 followed by ...h5, 24 e4 fxe4 25 e3 h5, 24 Kh2 Bg4, or 24 Qf3 g4 25 Qf2 c5 26 Rcd1 Be5 27 Qxf5 Bxg3 – in all cases Black has full compensation for the material. White’s king remains very exposed and his rooks have, as yet, little scope.

The text-move is effective because it not only defends g3 and e3, but also prepares to evacuate the king to the queenside if Black’s kingside threats become too dangerous.

24...Be5

White’s main problem is that there is little activity for his rooks. If he could penetrate with a rook to c7 and tie Black down to the defence of f7, then it would be hard for Black to proceed with his attack. Accurate play is necessary to achieve this.

25 Rh1?

Up to here White has played simply and directly, but now makes a serious error under the pressure imposed by Black’s sacrificial play. The alternatives provide a good illustration of the principles governing defence against a positional sacrifice:

1) 25 b4 gives White no advantage against accurate play, e.g.:

1a) 25...Bxg3+? 26 Kxg3 Qxe3+ 27 Kh2 Qf2+ 28 Kh1 and White wins.

1b) 25...h5?! 26 Rdc2 h4 (26...f4 27 gxf4 gxf4 28 Rg1+ Bg4 29 exf4 Bxf4 30 Ke1 also gives White a clear advantage) 27 gxh4 g4 (27...gxh4 loses to 28 Rxc7 Bg3+ 29 Kg1 Qxe3+ 30 Kh1) 28 Ke1 and the king escapes from the danger zone, when White is better.

1c) 25...g4! 26 Rd3 (26 Rdc2? Bxg3+ 27 Kxg3 Qxe3+ 28 Kh2 Qf2+ 29 Kh1 g3 wins for Black) 26...h5 gives Black enough counterplay.

2) 25 Rd3 and now:

2a) 25...h5 with a further branch:

2a1) 26 Qb7 h4 27 Rxc7 Bxg3+ 28 Kg1 Qe5 29 Rxf7 (29 Qd5 Bf2+ 30 Kh1 Qxd5+ 31 Rxd5 g4 looks dangerous for White after 32 Rxd6 g3 33 Rd1 Bxe3 or 32 Rxf5 g3 33 Rg5+ Kf8 34 Rb7 Be6) 29...d5 30 Rg7+ Qxg7 31 Qxd5+ Kh7 32 Qe6 draws.

2a2) 26 Qa8+! Kg7 27 Qb7 looks promising for White, as the line of ‘2a1’ would now lead to the f7-pawn being taken with check.

2b) 25...c5 26 Rh1 g4 gives Black fair compensation.

3) 25 Qd3 h5 26 Rh1 and now 26...Bg4 is unclear, but Black should avoid 26...g4 27 Qxf5 Bxg3+ 28 Kxg3 Qxe3+ 29 Kh4 Qxd2 30 Qg5+ Qxg5+ 31 Kxg5, which favours White.

So what is the correct move? When defending against a positional sacrifice, the key point is that the enemy attack arrives more slowly than after a direct sacrifice. Very often, part of the compensation for the material lies in the inability of the defender to make effective use of his extra material. In the current position, White’s problem is that he cannot easily get his rooks into play – but that shouldn’t have prevented him from trying!

4) 25 Rdc2! is the best continuation.

25...c5 26 b4 (White must be prepared to return some material in order to activate his rooks) 26...g4 (trying to disrupt White’s efforts by the threat of a sacrifice on g3) 27 Rc3! (now that Black has sealed his light-squared bishop out of the game with ...g4, it is definitely worth giving up a rook for the dark-squared bishop; in particular, once an endgame is reached, it almost doesn’t matter how many extra pawns Black has since he is playing without the bishop on h3) and now:

4a) 27...Bxc3 28 Rxc3 cxb4 (28...Qf6 29 Rd3 favours White) 29 Rc6 h5 30 Rxd6 and White stands very well.

4b) 27...h5 28 bxc5 h4 29 c6 hxg3+ 30 Kg1 Bxc3 31 Rxc3 Qc7 32 Rb3 wins for White.

4c) 27...cxb4 28 Rc7 Qf6 29 Rb7 f4 30 exf4 Bxf4 31 Rxf7 Qxf7 32 Qxf7+ Kxf7 33 gxf4 h5 34 Kg3 is a promising ending for White.

25...Bxg3+!

Suddenly the balance tips in Black’s favour. One careless move and Shirov is in his element.

26 Kxg3

After 26 Kf3 g4+ 27 Kxg3 Qxe3+ 28 Kh4 Qf2+ 29 Kh5 Qf4 30 Kh4 (30 Qa8+ Kg7 31 Qd8 g3 mates quickly) 30...Qg5+! 31 Kg3 f4+ 32 Kh2 Qh4 White is defenceless.

26...Qxe3+

27 Qf3

Not 27 Kh2? Qf2+ 28 Kxh3 g4#.

27...Qxd2

28 Qa8+

28 Rxh3 is no better, e.g. 28...g4 29 Qa8+ Kg7 and now:

1) 30 Rh2 Qe3+ 31 Kh4 (31 Kg2 Qxe2+ transposes) 31...Qe7+ 32 Kg3 Qe5+ 33 Kg2 Qxe2+ 34 Kg1 Qe1+ 35 Kg2 f4 wins for Black.

2) 30 Rh5 Qe1+ 31 Kh2 Qxe2+ 32 Kg3 (32 Qg2 loses to 32...g3+) 32...Qe1+ 33 Kg2 (33 Kh2 Kg6 is also hopeless for White) 33...f4 and Black wins.

3) 30 Rh1 Qe3+ 31 Kh2 Qxe2+ 32 Qg2 (32 Kg3 Qe3+ 33 Kg2 f4 34 Qxa6 Qe4+ 35 Kg1 g3 is winning for Black, while 32 Kg1 f4 33 Qg2 Qe1+ 34 Qf1 Qe4 35 Rh5 Kg6 36 Rh2 h5 is a familiar pattern) 32...Qe5+ 33 Kg1 Kg6 and White is powerless to prevent ...h5 followed by ...f4, with a deadly tidal wave of pawns. The material balance of five pawns against a rook rarely occurs in practice. In this case, the poor position of White’s rook and the security of Black’s king are the deciding factors.

28...Kg7

29 Kxh3

29 Rxh3 g4 transposes to the previous note.

29...Qxe2

Also good is 29...g4+ 30 Kh2 Qxe2+ 31 Qg2, transposing into the note to White’s 28th.

30 Qd5?!

This move should have lost at once, but there was no satisfactory alternative, e.g.:

1) 30 Qg2 g4+ 31 Kh2 Qe5+ 32 Kg1 again transposes to the note to White’s 28th move, which is very good for Black.

2) 30 Qc6 Qf2 31 Qc3+ Kg6 32 Qg3 Qxb2, with six pawns for the rook, is certainly very good for Black.

30...Kg6?!

30...Qf2 wins immediately.

31 Qd4?!

31 Qg2 was again the only chance, although after 31...g4+ 32 Kh2 Qe5+ 33 Kg1 h5 White has the same bad position as before, only this time a tempo down.

31...f4

There is no longer any defence.

32 Rg1

32...f5

0-1

This was an amazing tactical struggle, in which the players fought tooth and nail for the initiative. Shirov’s idea was highly creative, but owing to the relatively open nature of the position, White had the chance to create counterplay and this cast doubt on Shirov’s sacrifice. Had White found the correct continuation at move 25, then the game might have ended differently. However, one should never underestimate the practical side of chess. Whatever the objective assessment of Shirov’s idea, countering it over the board was fiendishly difficult.

The lessons here are:

1) Sacrifices can be made for long-term aims as well as immediate ones.

2) When defending against a positional sacrifice, generating counterplay is critical. A counter-sacrifice may be necessary to achieve this.

3) If the enemy king is sufficiently exposed, a queen plus a bunch of well-placed pawns can be enough to provide a strong attack.

4) Once you have started along the sacrificial path, there is usually no turning back.

All these lines are perfectly playable and, objectively speaking, there is little to choose between them.

A consistent and very combative move. Black does not mind having his pawns broken up if he can generate good piece activity. The quieter 11...e6 12 Nxf6+ Qxf6 13 Qd2 leaves White with a small but safe positional advantage after 13...Kh7 14 Rac1 Qe7 15 d5 Ne5 16 Nd4 or 13...g5 14 Rac1.

White accepts the offer to double Black’s pawns. This exchange is virtually forced sooner or later, as White’s d5-knight was being undermined.

12 Qd2 was played in a later game Timman-Shirov, Donner Memorial, Amsterdam 1995. Black obtained a satisfactory position after 12...bxc4 13 Nxf6+ exf6 14 Bxh6 Bxh6 15 Qxh6 Rxb2 16 Nh4 Ne7 and now Timman decided to force a draw by 17 Bd5 Nxd5 18 Nxg6 fxg6 19 Qxg6+ Kh8 20 Qh6+ with a repetition of moves.

White to play

In this position the doubled pawns constitute a weakness, albeit a slight one. It is not so much that the pawns are weak in themselves; it is more that without an e-pawn Black will find it hard to challenge White’s main central strongpoint, the d4-pawn. In compensation, the e-file is opened for Black’s rook and Black gains a little time, since White has exchanged a knight that has made three moves for a knight that has only moved once.
13 d5 Ne7 14 c5 is over-aggressive; after 14...Nf5 Black stands well.

Again, Black prefers activity over structure. It might seem more solid to play 13...axb5 keeping his queenside pawns together. The problem is that after 14 d5 Black has no good reply. For example, after 14...Ne5 (14...Ne7 15 Nd4 followed by Rc1 also starts to exert pressure against Black’s queenside) 15 Nd2, followed by Rc1, Black faces problems both with his queenside and with the almost trapped knight on e5. Thus playing 13...axb5 doesn’t really solve Black’s problems on the queenside, and offers no piece activity in compensation.

14 Qc1!? might be slightly more accurate. If Black plays 14...g5 (as in the game) then White can reply 15 Nxg5 fxg5 16 Bxc6 Bxc6 17 Qxc6 (17 a4 Rb6 18 a5 Rb4 19 Qxc6 Qc8 is unclear) 17...Rxb2 18 Qxa6 Qa8 19 Qc4 with a slight advantage.

14...Kh7 is also possible, but Shirov prefers to reduce the activity of White’s dark-squared bishop.

White to play

If White plays passively, then Black will continue ...Ne7-d5, occupying an excellent central square and preparing active play by ...f5. 15 Rac1 and 15 Ne1, for example, are both met by 15...Ne7 with satisfactory play for Black.

15 a4 is a critical move. Then:

1) 15...Rb8 16 d5 Ne7 (16...Ne5 17 Rfc1 also gives White an edge) 17 Nd4 leaves Black a little passively placed.

2) 15...Rb3! is more active. After 16 d5 Ne5 17 Rfc1 (17 Nd4 Nc4 18 Qc2 Nxe3 19 Qxb3 Nxf1 20 Rxf1 f5 activates the dark-squared bishop with a satisfactory position for Black) 17...Qb8 Black has sufficient counterplay.

15...Ne5 16 Rac1! (16 Nd4?! Nc4 17 Qc3 Nxe3 18 fxe3 f5 is fine for Black) 16...Nxf3+ 17 exf3 f5 18 b3 is uncomfortable for Black. White will play f4, when it is not clear what compensation Black has for his weak queenside pawns.
The natural follow-up. White has to move his knight in order to defend the d5-pawn, and after any other knight move Black achieves a good position with ...Nf5.
This sacrifice is the point behind Black’s play. Passively retreating the rook by 16...Rb8 would be an admission of failure; White would obtain an advantage with the natural move 17 Rac1.

White to play

Black has given up the exchange in return for a pawn. His compensation is not in the form of an immediate attack against the king, or any particularly strong threats. It is of a long-term nature, and is primarily positional rather than tactical. Such sacrifices are usually called ‘positional sacrifices’, although there is of course no clear-cut dividing line between positional sacrifices and those aiming for shorter-term compensation. Looking at the diagram, what has Black gained in return for his (admittedly fairly modest) material deficit? Firstly, he has eliminated White’s cramping central pawn, which both restrained his own c-pawn and created a possible outpost on c6. Secondly, he has removed White’s light-squared bishop. This not only gives White the immediate problem of dealing with the h3-pawn, but also raises the possibility that the weak light squares around the white king might eventually enable Black to start a direct attack.

Despite these advantages, the game is far from plain sailing for Black. His main problem is the vulnerability of his c- and d-pawns to attack along the half-open files. White’s rooks are well placed to swing into action, and Black could easily find himself facing strong pressure. Time is clearly an important factor here. If White has time to complete his development without Black having generated any active play, then Black will be forced back into a defensive posture. Therefore, both sides must try to make every tempo count. Objectively speaking, I believe that White has some advantage, but the resulting highly complex positions suit Shirov much better than Lautier, so while the balance of the position may still favour White, he is facing a tough challenge.

Lautier also knows how to play for the initiative, and is willing to offer his h-pawn in order to speed his rooks onto the central files. The alternatives are:

1) 18 Kh2 Qa8 (18...Nxe3? 19 fxe3! Qc8 20 g4 is bad, as White’s grip on f5 prevents Black from activating his dark-squared bishop) 19 Nc2 c5 keeps White’s pieces out of d4, and affords Black good compensation. He can continue with ...Re8, ...Bc6 and ...f5 (in some order) with plenty of activity, while White’s minor pieces have little scope.

2) 18 g4 Qc8 19 f3 (otherwise Black can at any rate force a draw by ...Bxg4) 19...Nxe3 20 Qxe3 f5 and with two active bishops against a knight and a weakened white kingside, Black is at least equal.

Black to play
Forced, because 18...Nxe3? 19 fxe3! sets up a blockade on f5.

20 Rfc1 is inferior. After 20...Be6 21 Qb7 f5 22 Rxc7 Bd5! 23 Qxd5 (23 Qa7 f4 24 Bb6 Qf6 gives Black dangerous threats, while after 23 Qb6 f4 24 Rd1 fxe3 25 Rxd5 exf2+ 26 Kxf2 Qf6+ 27 Kg2 Qe6 28 Rd2 Qe4+ 29 Kh2 Be5 White’s kingside is looking exposed) 23...Qxc7 it is up to White to demonstrate full equality.
Pinning the e3-bishop against the undefended e2-pawn.
This natural move completes White’s development. The alternative 21 Rd2 f5 22 Bd4 Bxd4 23 Qxd4 Qa8 24 f3 looks approximately equal.

White to play

Once again, Black must act quickly or else White will converge on the weak c7-pawn. This move not only threatens the b2-pawn, but also starts a general pawn advance in the direction of White’s king.

This move defends the e2- and b2-pawns in preparation for Bd4, and with very accurate play should give White an advantage. However, White can also gain an advantage with the simpler 22 Qc6. Then:

1) 22...f4 23 gxf4 gxf4 24 Bxf4 Bxb2 25 Rc4 is inadequate as Black is unable to exploit White’s slightly exposed king position. Note that 25...Rxe2? fails to 26 Qf3.

2) 22...Rxe3!? 23 fxe3 Qe7 is dubious here as White effectively has an extra tempo compared to the game. Although there are some traps to be negotiated, White should gain the advantage:

2a) 24 Rd3 Bxb2 25 Rc2 (25 Qxc7 Qe4 is good for Black) 25...Be5 26 Qxc7 Qf6 gives Black good play; for example, 27 Qc8+ (27 Kh2 g4 is similar) 27...Kg7 28 Kh2 g4 29 Rd5 (29 Qe8? Bxg3+! 30 Kxg3 Qa1 wins for Black) 29...h5 with a dangerous attack.

2b) 24 Kf2! Be5 (24...Bxb2 25 Qxc7 is very good for White) 25 Rd3 (25 Qxc7?? Bxg3+ mates) leaves Black struggling for compensation; for example, 25...Qf6 (25...h5 26 Qxc7 Qf6 27 Qd7 h4 28 gxh4 g4 29 Rc8+ Kg7 30 Qd8 defends) 26 Qe8+ Kg7 27 Rxc7 f4 28 exf4 gxf4 29 gxf4 Qxf4+ 30 Ke1 Qf1+ 31 Kd2 Bf4+ 32 Re3 and Black will end up the exchange for a pawn down.

3) 22...Bxb2 23 Rc2 Be5 24 Qxc7 and now:

Black to play

3a) 24...Qa8 25 Qc6 Qxc6 (25...f4 26 gxf4 gxf4 27 Qxa8 Rxa8 28 Bd4 is also slightly better for White) 26 Rxc6 Bxg3 27 Bd4 and White has the better ending.

3b) 24...Qxc7 25 Rxc7 Bxg3 (25...f4 26 gxf4 gxf4 27 Bd4 favours White) 26 Rd3 Be5 27 Bxg5 Bg4 (27...hxg5 28 Rxh3 and Black’s a-pawn is liable to be lost within a few moves) 28 Re7 (28 Bxh6 Bxe2 29 Re3 Bb5 30 Rf3 Kh7 31 Bf4 Kg6 is drawish) 28...Rc8 29 Bxh6 Bxe2 30 Rh3 and White retains a slight advantage. It is true that Black has two pawns for the exchange, but his pawns are weak and isolated. White is better, but the general reduction in the number of pawns means that Black retains fair drawing chances.

White to play

This second sacrifice ups the stakes. Black is willing to invest more material to increase his positional assets. Note that again the sacrifice does not lead to an immediate attack on the white king, so this could again be described as a ‘positional’ sacrifice. In this case, however, the possibility of an attack is closer than hitherto, since White’s shattered pawns and the ominous presence of the h3-bishop pose an obvious danger to the king.

Black has sufficient counterplay after the alternatives, e.g. 24 Qd3 Be5 25 Kh2 Bg4 followed by ...h5, 24 e4 fxe4 25 e3 h5, 24 Kh2 Bg4, or 24 Qf3 g4 25 Qf2 c5 26 Rcd1 Be5 27 Qxf5 Bxg3 – in all cases Black has full compensation for the material. White’s king remains very exposed and his rooks have, as yet, little scope.

The text-move is effective because it not only defends g3 and e3, but also prepares to evacuate the king to the queenside if Black’s kingside threats become too dangerous.

White to play

White’s main problem is that there is little activity for his rooks. If he could penetrate with a rook to c7 and tie Black down to the defence of f7, then it would be hard for Black to proceed with his attack. Accurate play is necessary to achieve this.

Up to here White has played simply and directly, but now makes a serious error under the pressure imposed by Black’s sacrificial play. The alternatives provide a good illustration of the principles governing defence against a positional sacrifice:

1) 25 b4 gives White no advantage against accurate play, e.g.:

1a) 25...Bxg3+? 26 Kxg3 Qxe3+ 27 Kh2 Qf2+ 28 Kh1 and White wins.

1b) 25...h5?! 26 Rdc2 h4 (26...f4 27 gxf4 gxf4 28 Rg1+ Bg4 29 exf4 Bxf4 30 Ke1 also gives White a clear advantage) 27 gxh4 g4 (27...gxh4 loses to 28 Rxc7 Bg3+ 29 Kg1 Qxe3+ 30 Kh1) 28 Ke1 and the king escapes from the danger zone, when White is better.

1c) 25...g4! 26 Rd3 (26 Rdc2? Bxg3+ 27 Kxg3 Qxe3+ 28 Kh2 Qf2+ 29 Kh1 g3 wins for Black) 26...h5 gives Black enough counterplay.

2) 25 Rd3 and now:

2a) 25...h5 with a further branch:

2a1) 26 Qb7 h4 27 Rxc7 Bxg3+ 28 Kg1 Qe5 29 Rxf7 (29 Qd5 Bf2+ 30 Kh1 Qxd5+ 31 Rxd5 g4 looks dangerous for White after 32 Rxd6 g3 33 Rd1 Bxe3 or 32 Rxf5 g3 33 Rg5+ Kf8 34 Rb7 Be6) 29...d5 30 Rg7+ Qxg7 31 Qxd5+ Kh7 32 Qe6 draws.

2a2) 26 Qa8+! Kg7 27 Qb7 looks promising for White, as the line of ‘2a1’ would now lead to the f7-pawn being taken with check.

2b) 25...c5 26 Rh1 g4 gives Black fair compensation.

3) 25 Qd3 h5 26 Rh1 and now 26...Bg4 is unclear, but Black should avoid 26...g4 27 Qxf5 Bxg3+ 28 Kxg3 Qxe3+ 29 Kh4 Qxd2 30 Qg5+ Qxg5+ 31 Kxg5, which favours White.

So what is the correct move? When defending against a positional sacrifice, the key point is that the enemy attack arrives more slowly than after a direct sacrifice. Very often, part of the compensation for the material lies in the inability of the defender to make effective use of his extra material. In the current position, White’s problem is that he cannot easily get his rooks into play – but that shouldn’t have prevented him from trying!

4) 25 Rdc2! is the best continuation.

Black to play

25...c5 26 b4 (White must be prepared to return some material in order to activate his rooks) 26...g4 (trying to disrupt White’s efforts by the threat of a sacrifice on g3) 27 Rc3! (now that Black has sealed his light-squared bishop out of the game with ...g4, it is definitely worth giving up a rook for the dark-squared bishop; in particular, once an endgame is reached, it almost doesn’t matter how many extra pawns Black has since he is playing without the bishop on h3) and now:

4a) 27...Bxc3 28 Rxc3 cxb4 (28...Qf6 29 Rd3 favours White) 29 Rc6 h5 30 Rxd6 and White stands very well.

4b) 27...h5 28 bxc5 h4 29 c6 hxg3+ 30 Kg1 Bxc3 31 Rxc3 Qc7 32 Rb3 wins for White.

4c) 27...cxb4 28 Rc7 Qf6 29 Rb7 f4 30 exf4 Bxf4 31 Rxf7 Qxf7 32 Qxf7+ Kxf7 33 gxf4 h5 34 Kg3 is a promising ending for White.
Suddenly the balance tips in Black’s favour. One careless move and Shirov is in his element.
After 26 Kf3 g4+ 27 Kxg3 Qxe3+ 28 Kh4 Qf2+ 29 Kh5 Qf4 30 Kh4 (30 Qa8+ Kg7 31 Qd8 g3 mates quickly) 30...Qg5+! 31 Kg3 f4+ 32 Kh2 Qh4 White is defenceless.
Not 27 Kh2? Qf2+ 28 Kxh3 g4#.
White to play

28 Rxh3 is no better, e.g. 28...g4 29 Qa8+ Kg7 and now:

1) 30 Rh2 Qe3+ 31 Kh4 (31 Kg2 Qxe2+ transposes) 31...Qe7+ 32 Kg3 Qe5+ 33 Kg2 Qxe2+ 34 Kg1 Qe1+ 35 Kg2 f4 wins for Black.

2) 30 Rh5 Qe1+ 31 Kh2 Qxe2+ 32 Kg3 (32 Qg2 loses to 32...g3+) 32...Qe1+ 33 Kg2 (33 Kh2 Kg6 is also hopeless for White) 33...f4 and Black wins.

3) 30 Rh1 Qe3+ 31 Kh2 Qxe2+ 32 Qg2 (32 Kg3 Qe3+ 33 Kg2 f4 34 Qxa6 Qe4+ 35 Kg1 g3 is winning for Black, while 32 Kg1 f4 33 Qg2 Qe1+ 34 Qf1 Qe4 35 Rh5 Kg6 36 Rh2 h5 is a familiar pattern) 32...Qe5+ 33 Kg1 Kg6 and White is powerless to prevent ...h5 followed by ...f4, with a deadly tidal wave of pawns. The material balance of five pawns against a rook rarely occurs in practice. In this case, the poor position of White’s rook and the security of Black’s king are the deciding factors.
29 Rxh3 g4 transposes to the previous note.

Also good is 29...g4+ 30 Kh2 Qxe2+ 31 Qg2, transposing into the note to White’s 28th.

White to play

This move should have lost at once, but there was no satisfactory alternative, e.g.:

1) 30 Qg2 g4+ 31 Kh2 Qe5+ 32 Kg1 again transposes to the note to White’s 28th move, which is very good for Black.

2) 30 Qc6 Qf2 31 Qc3+ Kg6 32 Qg3 Qxb2, with six pawns for the rook, is certainly very good for Black.
30...Qf2 wins immediately.
31 Qg2 was again the only chance, although after 31...g4+ 32 Kh2 Qe5+ 33 Kg1 h5 White has the same bad position as before, only this time a tempo down.
There is no longer any defence.

0-1

This was an amazing tactical struggle, in which the players fought tooth and nail for the initiative. Shirov’s idea was highly creative, but owing to the relatively open nature of the position, White had the chance to create counterplay and this cast doubt on Shirov’s sacrifice. Had White found the correct continuation at move 25, then the game might have ended differently. However, one should never underestimate the practical side of chess. Whatever the objective assessment of Shirov’s idea, countering it over the board was fiendishly difficult.

The lessons here are:

1) Sacrifices can be made for long-term aims as well as immediate ones.

2) When defending against a positional sacrifice, generating counterplay is critical. A counter-sacrifice may be necessary to achieve this.

3) If the enemy king is sufficiently exposed, a queen plus a bunch of well-placed pawns can be enough to provide a strong attack.

4) Once you have started along the sacrificial path, there is usually no turning back.
